nativeRange.habitat: Fast-flowing rocky stretches of the lower Rio Xingu, Pará state, Brazil; shelters in crevices and caves among large granite boulders in well-oxygenated, high-velocity channels
nativeRange.biotope: Rio Xingu rocky rapids (Volta Grande do Xingu)

decorPreferences: numerous caves and rocky crevices (clay or PVC tubes, stacked slate)
smooth or fine gravel substrate over sand
strong oxygenation and high water flow (powerheads, returns)
dim lighting or shaded areas to encourage daytime activity

diet.notes: Carnivore-leaning omnivore; prefers meaty protein over algae — unlike most plecos, Zebra Plecos are poor algae grazers. Feed high-quality sinking carnivore pellets, frozen/live bloodworms, brine shrimp, daphnia, and mysis shrimp. Supplement with occasional blanched vegetables (zucchini, cucumber) and algae wafers, though uptake may be low. Feed primarily after lights-out as this species is nocturnal.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras (cardinal tetras, rummynose tetras)
apistogramma and other small dwarf cichlids (with adequate caves)
other small non-aggressive bottom dwellers that won’t compete for caves
avoidWith: other Hypancistrus or plecos competing for the same cave territories
fast, boisterous fish that create stress
any fish large enough to harass or predate on plecos
snails (may be eaten in some cases)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Hypancistrus zebra; verify availability before use.

summary: The iconic L046 Zebra Pleco is a striking cave-dwelling loricariid from the Rio Xingu, Brazil, adorned with bold black-and-white stripes. IUCN Endangered; Brazil banned wild export in 2004. All legal trade specimens are captive-bred. High water flow and caves are non-negotiable; carnivore-leaning diet distinguishes it from algae-grazing plecos.
careNotes:
The Zebra Pleco demands warm (26–30°C), well-oxygenated, fast-flowing water that mimics its native Rio Xingu rapids. High flow is not optional — stagnant or sluggish water leads to rapid decline. Filtration should turn over the aquarium volume 10–15× per hour, and supplemental powerheads aimed along the substrate are strongly recommended. A temperature of 28°C is the practical sweet spot for activity and long-term health; temperatures below 26°C suppress immunity and appetite.

Unlike most plecos, Hypancistrus zebra is a poor algae grazer and should not be expected to clean tank glass. Its diet is carnivore-leaning: high-protein sinking pellets, frozen bloodworms, brine shrimp, mysis, and daphnia form the core menu. Feed after lights-out; daytime feeding is often refused. Uneaten food must be removed promptly — the species is sensitive to ammonia and nitrate accumulation. Weekly water changes of 25–30% are strongly advised.

Cave provision is mandatory: each Zebra Pleco should have at least one snug-fitting cave (the fish should be able to barely turn around inside). Commercial ceramic caves, clay tubes, or carefully stacked slate all work. Male territory centred on a cave is the norm; multiple caves reduce inter-male conflict in groups.

Due to IUCN Endangered status and the 2004 Brazilian export ban, all legally available specimens are captive-bred. Captive-bred fish are generally hardier and already accustomed to prepared foods, but still require the same warm, high-flow conditions as wild fish. Always verify captive-bred provenance when purchasing; prices of US $300–500+ per specimen reflect genuine rarity and the cost of responsible captive production.
breedingNotes:
Zebra Plecos are cave-spawners with paternal care, and are considered one of the more rewarding loricariids to breed once optimal conditions are met. The male occupies and defends a cave; when a receptive female enters, spawning occurs inside. Clutch size is small — typically 7–15 relatively large, adhesive eggs. The male guards the eggs and fans them with his fins for approximately 7 days until hatching. Fry remain in the cave, absorbing the yolk sac, for a further 5–7 days before becoming free-swimming.

Conditioning breeders with a varied high-protein diet (bloodworms, brine shrimp, quality pellets) and performing regular slightly-cooler water changes (simulating the seasonal Xingu flood pulse) are the key triggers reported to initiate spawning. Water temperature around 28–29°C and pH 6.5–7.0 are widely cited as optimal breeding parameters. Raise fry in a separate grow-out tank on micro-worm, newly hatched brine shrimp nauplii, and finely crushed high-protein pellets; maintain the same high-flow, high-oxygenation conditions as the adult tank.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Hypancistrus zebra
primary.notes: Primary source. Species profile covering natural habitat (Rio Xingu rocky rapids, Volta Grande), water parameters (26–30°C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 36–179 ppm), adult size (~10 cm), diet (carnivore-leaning, not an algae eater), breeding (cave-spawner, paternal care, 7–15 eggs, ~7-day incubation), conservation status (IUCN Endangered, 2004 Brazilian export ban), and husbandry notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Hypancistrus zebra
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Loricariidae, Siluriformes), adult TL (10 cm), native range (Rio Xingu, Brazil endemic), temperature range (26–30°C), IUCN Red List Endangered status, and threat notes including Belo Monte dam construction impact on the Volta Grande do Xingu habitat.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Zebra Pleco Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade-oriented care guide; confirmed carnivore-leaning diet, high-flow requirement, cave necessity, captive-bred sourcing guidance, intermediate care level, and typical retail pricing ($300–500+). Breeding notes (cave spawner, male guards eggs, small clutch) consistent with SeriouslyFish.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Hypancistrus zebra (L046)
additional.notes: UK hobbyist source; supplemented detail on the Belo Monte dam threat to wild populations, confirmed 2004 IBAMA export ban, noted that CB specimens now dominate legitimate trade. Also corroborated pH 6.5–7.5 range, temperature 28–30°C for breeding, and the importance of high O2 / turbulent flow.

additional.name: PlanetCatfish — Hypancistrus zebra L046
additional.notes: Specialist catfish database; confirmed cave-spawning behaviour, paternal egg-guarding, clutch size 7–15 eggs, incubation approximately 7 days, fry free-swimming 5–7 days post-hatch. Also noted inter-male aggression over cave territories and recommended one cave per male in group setups.

fish.breedingNotes: Cave-spawner with paternal care; male guards 7–15 eggs for ~7 days. Conditioning with high-protein live/frozen foods and periodic cool water changes trigger spawning. Requires species-specific cave dimensions. Fry raised on nauplii and micro-worm. Considered feasible but demanding; results improve significantly with dedicated breeding setups.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Brazil banned wild export in 2004 (IUCN Endangered Rio Xingu endemic); all legitimate trade specimens are captive-bred. Wild-caught smuggling remains a concern; verify CB origin from sellers.
